title: Longest Palindrome
description:
Given a string s (1 ≤ |s| ≤ 10^6) consisting of characters a–z, print any longest palindromic substring of s.

Input: a single line containing s.
Output: one longest palindromic substring. If there are several, you may print any.

Example:
Input: aybabtu
Output: bab

examples.explanation: bab is a palindrome and is the longest one in the string.

constraints: Characters are lowercase English letters a–z

mcqChain.questionText: Which of the following is a palindromic substring of "aybabtu"?
mcqChain.explanation: bab reads the same forwards and backwards. ayb and abt are not palindromes, and ayba (though a substring) is not symmetric.

wrongExplanations.1: ayb is not symmetric; its reverse is bya.
wrongExplanations.3: ayba is a substring but its reverse is abya, so it is not a palindrome.

mcqChain.hint: Check each option against its reverse.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abc", which of the following is a substring (contiguous) of s?
mcqChain.explanation: ab is contiguous in abc. ac is not contiguous, aa doesn't occur, and bca is not present.

wrongExplanations.0: ac is a subsequence but not contiguous in abc.
wrongExplanations.3: bca does not appear as a contiguous block in abc.

mcqChain.hint: Substrings are contiguous slices.

mcqChain.questionText: The palindrome "abba" is centered:
mcqChain.diagram:
a b b a
    ^ ^
 center is between the two b's
mcqChain.explanation: Even-length palindromes are centered between two characters. Odd-length palindromes are centered on a character.

wrongExplanations.1: Centering at a single 'b' gives odd length like 'bab', not 'abba'.

mcqChain.hint: Even length palindromes don't have a single middle character.

mcqChain.questionText: If we try expanding around all possible centers (characters and gaps), how many centers are there for a string of length n?
mcqChain.explanation: There are n character centers and n-1 gap centers, totaling 2n-1.

wrongExplanations.0: This counts only character centers, missing gaps.
wrongExplanations.3: There is no gap after the last character, so it's 2n-1, not 2n.

mcqChain.hint: Count characters and gaps separately.

mcqChain.questionText: Checking all substrings (≈n(n+1)/2) and verifying each in O(n) time leads to what time complexity?
mcqChain.explanation: There are O(n^2) substrings and each check is O(n), so O(n^3).

wrongExplanations.0: That's the count of substrings but ignores the check cost.

mcqChain.hint: Multiply the number of substrings by the per-check cost.

mcqChain.questionText: Expanding around each of the 2n-1 centers can take how long in the worst case (e.g., s = "aaaa...a")?
mcqChain.explanation: Each center can expand O(n) in the worst case, across O(n) centers → O(n^2).

wrongExplanations.0: That's the total sum when reusing information cleverly, not when expanding independently.

mcqChain.hint: Consider a string of all equal characters.

mcqChain.questionText: Given n up to 10^6 and a 1 s time limit, is an O(n^2) approach likely to finish in time?

options.label: Likely too slow; we need near-linear time
Yes, because typical inputs are random

mcqChain.explanation: O(n^2) at n=10^6 implies ~10^12 steps, which is too large for 1s. Aim for near O(n).

wrongExplanations.0: Constant factors don't overcome a quadratic growth at this scale.

mcqChain.hint: Estimate n^2 when n=10^6.

mcqChain.questionText: Suppose we know a palindrome centered at C reaching right boundary R. For a position i with i < R, what is a safe initial radius at i using its mirror i' = 2C - i?
mcqChain.diagram:
L ... i' ... C ... i ... R
Use the known palindrome to bound radius at i.

options.label: Start with P[i] = min(R - i, P[i'])
Start with P[i] = R - i + P[i']

mcqChain.explanation: Inside the known right boundary, the radius at i cannot exceed R-i and cannot exceed the mirrored radius. Take the minimum, then try to expand.

wrongExplanations.0: It can be cut off by the right boundary; using P[i'] blindly can overrun R.

mcqChain.hint: Two limits: mirror radius and how close i is to R.

mcqChain.questionText: Why insert a separator (e.g., '#') between characters before processing?
mcqChain.diagram:
s:  a b a
T: # a # b # a #

options.label: It makes every palindrome have a single center (unifies odd and even cases)
It reduces the number of characters to process
It ensures lexicographic order of characters
It increases the maximum possible radius values

mcqChain.explanation: With separators, all palindromes become odd-length around a single center, simplifying uniform handling.

wrongExplanations.1: It actually increases the length by roughly 2x, not decreases.

mcqChain.hint: Think about handling even-length palindromes uniformly.

mcqChain.questionText: Transform s = "aba" by inserting '#' between characters and at both ends (without special end markers). Which is correct?
mcqChain.explanation: #a#b#a# places separators between every adjacent pair and at both ends.

wrongExplanations.1: Missing separators at the ends; uniform centering is lost.
wrongExplanations.3: Too many separators; not one between each adjacent pair.

mcqChain.hint: There should be one separator between any two original characters and one at each end.

mcqChain.questionText: Why add distinct sentinels (like '^' and '$') at the beginning and end of the transformed string?

options.label: To avoid explicit bounds checks during expansion
To change the longest palindrome length
To enable binary search on centers

mcqChain.explanation: Unique sentinels guarantee expansions stop before going out of range, removing index checks.

wrongExplanations.1: They don't change which palindromes exist in s; they just simplify expansion.

mcqChain.hint: What happens when comparing beyond the ends if the boundary characters are distinct?

mcqChain.questionText: If |s| = 3 and T = '^#' + '#'.join(s) + '#$', what is |T|?
mcqChain.explanation: T has length 2n+3, so 2*3+3=9.

wrongExplanations.2: Off by one; re-count the characters in '^#a#b#a#$'.

mcqChain.hint: Count '^', '$', all characters, and all separators.

mcqChain.questionText: Given a center C and position i, what is the index of i's mirror across C in the transformed string?
mcqChain.explanation: Mirroring around C means reflecting the offset: i' = C - (i - C) = 2C - i.

wrongExplanations.3: This reflects C across i, not i across C.

mcqChain.hint: Compute i' so that (i - C) = -(i' - C).

mcqChain.questionText: After computing maxLen and centerIndex in the transformed string, what is the start index in the original string s?
mcqChain.diagram: start = ? given centerIndex and maxLen on T with '#'
mcqChain.explanation: Each original character maps to two positions in T (with a separator), so divide by 2 after subtracting the radius.

wrongExplanations.1: The '+1' shift is not needed when sentinels are '^#' and '#$'.

mcqChain.hint: Consider how many T positions correspond to one s position.

mcqChain.questionText: Read the input string efficiently.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading a single line and stripping the newline gives the exact string. Other options either include the newline or return the wrong type.

wrongExplanations.1: Leaves the trailing newline, which would break character comparisons.
wrongExplanations.3: split() returns a list, not the raw string.

mcqChain.hint: Ensure the trailing newline is removed.

mcqChain.questionText: Construct the transformed string T, and prepare its length N and array P.
mcqChain.explanation: Separators between all characters and distinct sentinels at both ends are needed. P must match len(T).

wrongExplanations.3: Missing separators between characters and P has the wrong length.

mcqChain.hint: Use '#'.join(s) and add '^#' at the start and '#$' at the end.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the current center C and right boundary R.
mcqChain.explanation: Start with no known palindrome: both center and right boundary at the very start sentinel.

wrongExplanations.1: Starting inside the string is arbitrary and breaks mirror logic before any expansion.

mcqChain.hint: No palindrome is known before processing.

mcqChain.questionText: Set up the main loop over centers and seed P[i] using the mirror where applicable.
mcqChain.explanation: Iterate excluding sentinels. Mirror is 2*C - i. Inside R, seed P[i] with min(R - i, P[mir]).

wrongExplanations.1: range(N) includes sentinels and P[mir] may exceed the right boundary.
wrongExplanations.3: min(R, P[mir]) ignores how close i is to R; must be R - i.

mcqChain.hint: Avoid the very first and last positions; they are sentinels.

mcqChain.questionText: Expand around i as long as characters match.

options.label: while T[i + 1 + P[i]] == T[i - 1 - P[i]]:\n        P[i] += 1
while T[i + P[i]] == T[i - P[i]]:\n        P[i] += 1
while i + P[i] < R and T[i + 1 + P[i]] == T[i - 1 - P[i]]:\n        P[i] += 1
while T[i + 1 + P[i]] == T[i - P[i]]:\n        P[i] += 1

mcqChain.explanation: Compare symmetric characters just outside the current radius. Sentinels ensure we won't go out of bounds.

wrongExplanations.1: Off by one; this starts comparing the center with itself.
wrongExplanations.3: Asymmetric indices; not comparing matched offsets.

mcqChain.hint: Start comparing at one step beyond the current radius on both sides.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
while T[i + 1 + P[i]] == T[i - 1 - P[i]]:
        P[i] += 1

mcqChain.questionText: If the palindrome at i extends beyond the current right boundary, update center and right boundary.

options.label: if i + P[i] > R:\n        C = i\n        R = i + P[i]
if i + P[i] >= R:\n        R = i + P[i]
if i - P[i] < R:\n        C = i\n        R = P[i]
if i + P[i] > R:\n        C = R\n        R = i

mcqChain.explanation: When the right edge extends, set the new center to i and R to i+P[i].

wrongExplanations.1: Not updating C breaks future mirror usage; also updating on equality isn't necessary.
wrongExplanations.3: Swapping these breaks the meaning of C and R.

mcqChain.hint: Center should move to i when its palindrome reaches farthest right.

mcqChain.questionText: Scan P to find the maximum radius and its center, then extract and print the answer.

options.label:
maxLen = 0
centerIndex = 0
for i in range(1, N - 1):
    if P[i] > maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = (centerIndex - maxLen) // 2
print(s[start:start + maxLen])

maxLen = 1
centerIndex = 1
for i in range(N):
    if P[i] >= maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = centerIndex // 2
print(s[start:start + maxLen])

maxLen = 0
centerIndex = 0
for i in range(0, N):
    if P[i] > maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = (centerIndex - 2*maxLen) // 2
print(s[start:centerIndex])

maxLen = 0
centerIndex = 0
for i in range(1, N - 1):
    if P[i] > maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = centerIndex // 2 - maxLen
print(T[start:start + maxLen])

mcqChain.explanation: Search excluding sentinels. The mapping back is start = (centerIndex - maxLen)//2 and the answer is s[start:start+maxLen].

wrongExplanations.1: Using >= changes tie-breaking and start = centerIndex//2 ignores the radius.
wrongExplanations.3: This slices T instead of s and uses a wrong start formula.

mcqChain.hint: Remember the mapping from transformed indices back to s.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
maxLen = 0
centerIndex = 0
for i in range(1, N - 1):
    if P[i] > maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = (centerIndex - maxLen) // 2
print(s[start:start + maxLen])

mcqChain.questionText: If the input is "cbbd", what will the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: The longest palindromic substring is "bb". Single-letter palindromes exist but are shorter.

wrongExplanations.2: A valid palindrome but not the longest.

mcqChain.hint: Check all palindromic substrings: c, b, bb, d.

mcqChain.questionText: For input "aaaaa", what is the output?
mcqChain.explanation: The whole string is a palindrome, so the longest is the entire string.

wrongExplanations.1: This is palindromic but not the longest.

mcqChain.hint: A run of identical letters is a palindrome itself.

mcqChain.questionText: On the sample input "aybabtu", which palindrome will the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: Both "bab" and "aba" are palindromes, but "bab" appears and is the one shown in the example. Any longest one is acceptable.

wrongExplanations.1: In aybabtu, "aba" does not occur as a contiguous substring.

mcqChain.hint: Trace substrings around y/b/a.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of this approach with the transformed string and reuse of mirror information?
mcqChain.explanation: Each position expands only as far as it contributes new characters beyond the current right boundary, summing to linear time.

wrongExplanations.2: Independent expansion would be quadratic, but reuse bounds it to linear.

mcqChain.hint: Count total successful character comparisons across all centers.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space usage of this method (beyond the input string)?
mcqChain.explanation: The transformed string T has size ≈2n+3 and P has size ≈2n+3, both linear.

wrongExplanations.1: We store arrays proportional to n.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the sizes of T and P.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge cases are correctly handled by this method and code?

options.label: Single-character input and multiple equal-length longest palindromes

mcqChain.explanation: Separators unify odd/even palindromes and we return any longest one. Single-character inputs trivially work.

wrongExplanations.3: Input is defined as a–z only; other characters are outside constraints.

mcqChain.hint: Does the separator trick cover both odd and even cases?

finalCodeSnippets:
while T[i + 1 + P[i]] == T[i - 1 - P[i]]:
        P[i] += 1

maxLen = 0
centerIndex = 0
for i in range(1, N - 1):
    if P[i] > maxLen:
        maxLen = P[i]
        centerIndex = i
start = (centerIndex - maxLen) // 2
print(s[start:start + maxLen])

reasoningChain.question: How many centers should we consider (including gaps) and what does a palindrome center mean for odd/even cases?
reasoningChain.insight: There are 2n-1 centers; even palindromes are centered between characters and odd palindromes on a character.

reasoningChain.question: Why are naive approaches too slow for n up to 10^6?
reasoningChain.insight: Checking all substrings is O(n^3) and expanding independently around each center is O(n^2), both infeasible for the constraints.

reasoningChain.question: How can we reuse information when scanning centers from left to right?
reasoningChain.insight: Track the rightmost palindrome (center C, right boundary R). For i<R, seed P[i] with min(R - i, P[mirror]).

reasoningChain.question: How do we unify odd and even palindromes and avoid bounds checks?
reasoningChain.insight: Transform s into T by inserting separators and distinct sentinels '^' and '$' so every palindrome has a single center and expansions stop at sentinels.

reasoningChain.question: What are the exact update and expansion rules?
reasoningChain.insight: Expand while T[i+1+P[i]] == T[i-1-P[i]]. If i+P[i] > R, set C=i and R=i+P[i].

reasoningChain.question: How do we map the result back to the original string?
reasoningChain.insight: Find maxLen and centerIndex in P; start = (centerIndex - maxLen)//2; answer is s[start:start+maxLen].
